title: Texas Abolitionists Make Abortion's Abolition an Official TX GOP Priority
author: Michael Lockwood
orientation: Reports that the Republican Party of Texas, at its 2020 biennial convention, adopted the complete abolition of abortion as one of eight official legislative priorities, with abolitionist delegates outnumbering incrementalist witnesses five-to-one and successfully defending 'equal protection' language in the priority's text.
audiences: State legislator weighing the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act

questions.question: What exactly did the Republican Party of Texas adopt regarding abortion at its biennial convention?
questions.answer: The RPT passed a legislative priority calling for the complete abolition of abortion by guaranteeing the right to life and equal protection of the laws to every preborn child from fertilization onward.
questions.quote: During their recent biennial convention, the Republican Party of Texas (RPT) passed a legislative priority to abolish abortion completely. It reads: “Abolish abortion by ensuring the right to life and equal protection of the laws to all preborn children from the moment of fertilization.”

questions.question: How decisively did the abolitionist position prevail over incrementalism at the convention hearings?
questions.answer: Abolitionists outnumbered incrementalists testifying at the hearings by five-to-one, and the resulting abolition priority became one of the party's eight official priorities, while the competing incremental proposal failed to crack the top fifteen.
questions.quote: Despite Texas Right to Life and Texas Alliance for Life’s insistence on an incremental approach to ending abortion, the RPT chose to listen to the abolitionists, who outnumbered those testifying in favor of incrementalism five-to-one at the hearings. The complete abolition of abortion is now one of Texas Republicans’ eight official legislative priorities, while the incremental proposal did not even make the top fifteen.

questions.question: What argument did delegate David Sims make against the prevailing pro-life strategy?
questions.answer: Sims told the Legislative Priorities Committee that begging the Supreme Court for permission to compromise with evil is unprincipled and ineffective, and that Texas must instead stand on Scripture and the Constitution to extend equal protection against murder to preborn children.
questions.quote: “We’ve got to stop with the strategy of begging the supreme court to allow us to compromise with evil. It’s unprincipled and it clearly isn’t working. It’s time to stand firmly on God’s word and on the Constitution and provide equal protection of the laws against murder to preborn children. It’s time to abolish abortion.”

questions.question: How did Wesley Thomas respond to fears that abolishing abortion in Texas could trigger civil war?
questions.answer: Thomas reframed the question by pointing out that more than 55,000 children are aborted in Texas each year, arguing that war is already underway and that refusing to risk our own lives to stop it amounts to demanding that those children die in our place.
questions.quote: “55,000 plus children per year are currently being aborted… That *is* a war, okay? And so… if we’re not willing to lay down our lives to save 55,000 children, are we not asking them to lay down their lives for us instead?”

questions.question: What does delegate David Bellow's mid-convention conversion suggest about the appeal of abolitionism?
questions.answer: Bellow heard the abolitionist testimonies, was persuaded on the spot, and offered his own testimony declaring that an unborn person has a right to life from conception regardless of federal opinion, illustrating that Christians not pre-poisoned by pro-life slander tend to embrace the abolitionist call.
questions.quote: “We need to say… that an unborn person is a person and has a right to life from conception, and if the federal government wants to say otherwise, who cares?” Bellow’s on-the-spot conversion is a testament to the growth of abolitionism. When Christians who haven’t already been fed slander about abolitionists by pro-life leaders hear the call of abolition, they are often persuaded to the cause.

questions.question: Why was the fight over keeping the words 'equal protection' in the priority so significant?
questions.answer: A committee member moved to strike 'equal protection' so the priority would call for some lesser protection of preborn children rather than treating them as legal equals; abolitionist J.R. Haas argued that denying equal protection to persons is intolerable before God and the Constitution, and the motion was withdrawn, exposing how readily incrementalists were willing to deny preborn children equal standing under law.
questions.quote: “He is saying that we should deny equal protection to preborn children. Friends, I don’t know about you, but I have a real problem with that,” Haas said. “Do we believe that these babies are persons? If so, we must not, before God and under our Constitution, deny them the equal protection of our laws. Please do not strike these two words. Thank you.”

questions.question: What concession did incrementalists actually win in the final wording?
questions.answer: They successfully removed the explicit language calling for defiance of Roe and Casey, though Lockwood notes that the priority still implicitly defies the Supreme Court because abortion cannot be abolished from conception without doing so.
questions.quote: Incrementalists were able to strike other language from the legislative priority however, most notably the wording which explicitly called for *Roe* and *Casey* to be defied. The language of the bill still implies defiance of the supreme court, however, as abortion cannot be abolished from conception without defying the supreme court.

questions.question: What practical pressure does this legislative priority put on Texas state representatives?
questions.audience: State legislator weighing the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act
questions.answer: According to Rep. Briscoe Cain, the priorities function as commands from the grassroots, so legislators who refuse to support the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act will face constituents who can point directly to the official Republican legislative priorities and demand to know why.
questions.quote: “With abolition being a clearly-written legislative priority, I expect more legislators to sign on [to The Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act]. They may do so willingly or because there’s a mandate from the grassroots… I think legislators who don’t support it will find many upset constituents who are able to point to the Texas Republican legislative priorities and say ‘why didn’t you support this?’ After the legislative session, Republican legislators who rebuffed the grassroots will have a lot of questions to answer.”

questions.question: What happened the last time the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act came up for consideration?
questions.answer: Two years earlier, the bill drew 11 cosponsors and over 300 supportive witnesses in a hearing that ran until almost 3 a.m., yet committee chairman Jeff Leach refused to grant it a vote, leaving abortion legal in Texas and resulting in the legal killing of an estimated 110,000 preborn children.
questions.quote: Two years ago, the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act had 11 cosponsors and over 300 people who testified in favor of it, causing the hearing to last until 2:58AM. Nevertheless, committee chairman Jeff Leach denied the bill a vote, keeping abortion legal in Texas for two more years and guaranteeing 110,000 preborn children would be legally murdered.

questions.question: What position has new RPT chairman Allen West taken on a bill of abolition?
questions.audience: State legislator weighing the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act
questions.answer: West has publicly committed that he would speak in full support of any bill that abolishes abortion and saves the lives of unborn babies beginning at conception.
questions.quote: “If a bill were introduced that would abolish abortion – saving the lives of unborn babies, beginning at conception – I would speak in full support.”

questions.question: What does the author argue Texas must finally do, and on what authority?
questions.answer: Lockwood calls Texas to abandon incremental strategies and oppose unjust court rulings by extending equal protection to every preborn human, framing this duty as commanded by God, the U.S. Constitution, and the Texas Constitution alike.
questions.quote: It’s time for Texas to stop with the fruitless incremental strategies and do what it should have done from the very beginning: oppose unjust court rulings and provide equal protection for all preborn humans as mandated by God, the US Constution, and the Texas Constitution.
